questions.prompt: What's the price difference for batched requests vs. real-time API on Claude?

choices.text: ~50% off per-token rates (both input and output). Batches process up to 100,000 requests with a 24-hour SLA — typically completing in minutes-to-hours. Stacks with prompt caching for compounding savings on workloads with stable prefixes.

questions.explanation: Batches trade latency for cost — 50% off in exchange for async processing within 24 hours. Perfect for analytics, backfills, eval runs, content generation at scale. Combine with caching when prompts share a stable prefix for near-free repeat input.

questions.prompt: A team tries to use the Batches API for their user-facing chat. Users wait minutes for replies. What happened?

choices.text: Batch is fundamentally async — minimum overhead is a few minutes, upper bound is 24 hours. It's the wrong tool for real-time UX. Revert to the standard Messages API for user-facing requests; reserve batches for analytics, backfills, evals, and other offline workloads.

questions.explanation: Batch's value is offline processing at half price. The 24-hour SLA is the upper bound; minimum overhead is still minutes. For any flow where a user is watching, use the regular API. If your team is tempted to 'batch real-time' to save money, the right move is caching + Haiku-tier routing, not batching.
